g.text: Immutability, StringBuilder, StringBuffer, and Performance Optimization

g.data-narration: Understanding string modification is crucial because strings are immutable in Java, meaning every modification creates a new object in memory.
This module covers three main string classes: immutable String for fixed values, StringBuilder for fast single-thread modifications, and StringBuffer for thread-safe operations.
Strings are immutable objects - once created, their value cannot be changed. Any operation that appears to modify a string actually creates a new string object.
g.text: In Java, String is immutable - once created, its value cannot be modified.
s.concat(" World"); // Returns NEW string, s remains "Hello"

g.data-narration: Java provides three ways to concatenate strings: the + operator, concat() method, and StringBuilder's append() method. Each has different performance characteristics.
The substring method extracts portions of a string using start and end indices. The replace method substitutes characters or sequences, returning new strings.
g.text: Both return new String, original unchanged

g.data-narration: Case modification methods like toUpperCase() and toLowerCase() convert string case. The trim() method removes leading and trailing whitespace.
The String.valueOf() method converts various types to String representation - primitives, objects, or char arrays become their string equivalents.
Here's a comprehensive comparison of String, StringBuilder, and StringBuffer - the three classes differ in mutability, thread safety, and performance.
Using string concatenation with + in loops creates a new String object each iteration, leading to O(n²) time complexity and excessive memory usage.
g.text: Example 1: BAD Approach - String in Loop

g.data-narration: StringBuilder provides O(n) performance by using a resizable array. The append method modifies the existing object without creating new strings each iteration.
String methods can be chained since each returns a new String. This creates a processing pipeline where trim, toUpperCase, and replace execute in sequence.
StringBuffer uses synchronized methods making it thread-safe. Multiple threads can safely modify the same StringBuffer without data corruption.
Remember these key differences: String for constants (immutable), StringBuilder for single-thread modifications (fast), StringBuffer for multi-threaded code (synchronized).
For exams, remember: start with immutability, avoid + in loops, use StringBuilder, prefer StringBuffer only when thread safety is explicitly required.

g.text: Always state String's immutable nature first
Avoid + operator in loops - use StringBuilder

g.data-narration: In summary, strings are immutable in Java - modifications create new objects. Use StringBuilder for efficiency in loops, StringBuffer for thread-safe operations, and String for constant values.
g.text: Strings are immutable - modifications create new objects. Choose StringBuilder for loops,
StringBuffer for thread-safety, and String for fixed values.
